description: Stored procedure for inventory reservation. Application calls reserve_inventory() under READ COMMITTED isolation (default) and 6000 reservation requests/sec for hot SKUs. Review correctness and concurrency.
review_checklist: Identify check-then-act race conditions under READ COMMITTED
Distinguish row-level locks needed (FOR UPDATE vs SHARE)
Audit isolation level interaction with the function
Consider optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency tradeoffs

ground_truth.description: Classic check-then-act race condition. Two concurrent calls for the same SKU can both read `available = 10`, both compare `available >= 5`, both UPDATE reserved = reserved + 5 — the second UPDATE is incrementally applied to the now-15 value, NOT serialized against the first's read. Result: oversold inventory. Fix: use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the inventory row to acquire a row-level lock for the duration of the transaction: `SELECT on_hand - reserved INTO available FROM inventory WHERE sku = p_sku FOR UPDATE;`

Function does not declare isolation. Under READ COMMITTED (default), the SELECT and UPDATE can read different snapshots even with FOR UPDATE — the UPDATE re-reads the row but the available variable from the SELECT may now be stale. Two safe options: (a) use SELECT FOR UPDATE + check available in the same statement: `UPDATE inventory SET reserved = reserved + p_qty WHERE sku = p_sku AND on_hand - reserved >= p_qty RETURNING ...`, or (b) request SERIALIZABLE isolation level explicitly.

FOR UPDATE on hot SKU rows serializes ALL concurrent reservations on that SKU. At 6000 reservations/sec for popular items (Black Friday concert tickets, limited drops), this becomes a queue. Alternative pattern: use SKIP LOCKED with reservation tokens (each reservation is a separate insertable row, no UPDATE on shared inventory state); or use CRDTs (G-Counter for reserved counts).

Function returns BOOLEAN with no information about WHY a reservation failed. For 'inventory full' vs 'sku not found' the caller has no way to distinguish. Return a structured result: `RETURNS TABLE(success BOOLEAN, reason VARCHAR)` or similar.

Architectural pattern: optimistic concurrency via the existing `version` column is a better fit for this problem. The version column is already there but unused. Pattern: SELECT version, on_hand, reserved → check available → UPDATE inventory SET reserved=reserved+qty, version=version+1 WHERE sku=? AND version=?. If row count = 0, retry. No row locks, much higher throughput, well-suited to e-commerce reservation flows.

Schema split: inventory should likely be partitioned by SKU bucket or shard to spread hot SKU load across multiple physical rows/partitions. For top-100 SKUs that get 80% of traffic (Pareto), maintain N replicas of the row (sku|bucket as PK), distribute reservation requests across buckets, sum at query time.
